narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a full-time study plan and a lower-middle family income background, we understand how disappointing it must be to receive this law school admission decision. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated an 82.8% chance that you would fail the bar exam—far too high a risk for approval, leading to the rejection of your application. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, especially your position in the lowest decile for grades in Year 3, which stood out as the strongest factor pushing the model toward forecasting failure. Your Year 1 grades, also in the lowest decile, reinforced this concern as the second-most influential element, signaling early struggles that the model sees as persistent hurdles to bar success. Compared to typical students—who average mid-level deciles around the 5th or 6th for both years—your rankings are notably lower, making these the top reasons the model leaned heavily against you. Your LSAT score of 28, well below the dataset average of about 37, added further weight toward the failure prediction as the third key factor. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 2.10, though below the average of 3.21, slightly nudged the prediction toward passing, offering a small counterbalance. Your full-time enrollment status and family income also provided minor positive influences, aligning with the majority of successful students in the data. Overall, these academic metrics painted a picture riskier than most applicants, tipping the scales decisively. While this stings, it's not the end of your path to law—consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score significantly, strengthening your grades through additional coursework, or exploring part-time programs or gap-year experiences to build a stronger profile. Many students in similar situations have improved their standings and succeeded later; we encourage you to seek advising from pre-law counselors for targeted next steps. You're capable of turning this around with focused effort.
